title: The Land That Remembered Itself as Paradise
bullets: Al-Andalus is the one place three modern groups need to have been a paradise and a fourth needs to have been a tyranny, which is why almost no history of it is neutral.

The convivencia debate runs from Menocal's romantic Ornament of the World against Fernandez-Morera's Myth of the Andalusian Paradise, behind them the older Castro versus Sanchez-Albornoz quarrel over what Spain essentially is.
The Strait of Gibraltar is only about eight miles wide, so for eight centuries Iberia was the one place the Islamic world and Latin Christendom shared a crossable land border.
Even the name is unsettled: the traditional derivation from the Vandals is now doubted, with Gothic and pre-Roman origins proposed for the single most-argued-about word in the story.
The world of 700 had a shattered Mediterranean, a Byzantine Empire that had just lost Syria and Egypt fast, and an eighty-year-old Umayyad Caliphate stretching from India's borders to Morocco.
Most of the men who conquered Spain in 711 were not Arabs but recently Islamized Berbers, whose languages are as distant from Arabic as Finnish is from English.
Iberia in 711 was ruled by the Visigoths, a thin Germanic warrior aristocracy over a Hispano-Roman Christian and Jewish majority, and it was in the middle of a succession war.

title: Eight Years That Took Eight Centuries to Undo
bullets: In 711 Tariq ibn Ziyad crossed with perhaps 7,000 mostly Berber troops and landed at the rock that still bears his name, Jabal Tariq, which English mangled into Gibraltar.
King Roderic's army disintegrated at the Battle of Guadalete and Roderic vanished from history; the interesting question is why one lost battle ended an entire kingdom.
The Visigothic state was a thin warrior aristocracy nobody had reason to defend, so for most inhabitants the conquest was less a catastrophe than a change of management.
The Treaty of Tudmir of 713 survives in quotation: a real surrender pact letting the conquered keep their churches and faith for tribute and the subordinate dhimmi status.
The dhimma was a tax-and-status bargain, not forced conversion and not modern tolerance, and most arguments about al-Andalus are really arguments about how to feel about it.
The Battle of Tours in 732 was later mythologized by Gibbon as the battle that saved Christendom, though modern historians see a large raid, not an invasion of France.
Pelagius's skirmish at Covadonga around 722 grew into the Kingdom of Asturias, the acorn from which the 770-year Reconquista myth was later back-projected.
The same year as Tariq's crossing, the Umayyad Caliphate was also conquering Sind on the Indus, punching into two continents at once.

title: The Falcon of Córdoba
bullets: In 750 the Abbasid Revolution overthrew and massacred the Umayyad dynasty in Damascus, the event that split the histories of the Islamic east and west.
Abd al-Rahman I, the lone Umayyad survivor, fled the width of the Islamic world and in 756 founded the independent Emirate, still nominally naming the Abbasid caliph in Friday prayers.
Later tradition has the Abbasid caliph al-Mansur call him the Falcon of the Quraysh, the one Umayyad who got away and built a kingdom at the other end of the world.
In 785 he began the Great Mosque on a church over a Roman temple, modeled on the lost Umayyad mosque of Damascus: he was building Syria in Spain.
Charlemagne's rearguard was annihilated at Roncesvalles in 778 by Christian Basques, an ambush rewritten three centuries later as the Song of Roland, a holy war it never was.
Roncesvalles is the first proof of the narrative's recurring pattern: what happened in al-Andalus and what people needed to have happened are different stories, and the second is more famous.
Charlemagne's frontier hardened into the Marca Hispanica after Barcelona fell in 801, the seed of Catalonia and the Crown of Aragon that would finish the story.

title: Martyrs, Rebels, and the Slow Conversion
bullets: Around four dozen Christians sought execution between 850 and 859 by publicly denouncing Muhammad; the martyrs were a protest against assimilation, not against persecution.
Paul Alvarus lamented that his fellow Christians could no longer write a Latin letter but turned out polished Arabic verse, while in the same century Frankish literacy had nearly collapsed.
Umar ibn Hafsun fortified the mountain stronghold of Bobastro and defied the Emirate for forty years, at one point nearly bringing the state down.
Vikings sacked Seville in 844, and between Ibn Hafsun, the Banu Qasi, and other revolts the Emirate by 900 effectively controlled little more than Cordoba.
Richard Bulliet's conversion S-curve suggests Muslims became a majority only around the year 1000, so for its first three centuries Islamic Spain was mostly non-Muslim.
Conversion ran on incentive, prestige, intermarriage, and time, not on the sword, since the pact system explicitly forbade forced conversion.
The Christians who stayed under Muslim rule and Arabized became the Mozarabs, a culture that was neither the Visigothic past nor the Muslim present.

title: The Caliph
bullets: Abd al-Rahman III inherited at twenty-one a state that barely ran Cordoba and over a 49-year reign made it the most powerful in western Europe.
By 929 he proclaimed himself Caliph, a strategic counter to the Fatimid claim across the strait, so the Mediterranean now had three rival caliphs at once.
From 936 he built Madinat al-Zahra, a purpose-built palace-city terraced in three levels of escalating splendor to make caliphal power legible to any visitor.
The Caliphate minted a gold dinar of dependable full weight, trusted across the western Mediterranean, a reliable sign its revenues and credibility were both high.
Hasdai ibn Shaprut, a Jewish physician, became the caliph's effective foreign minister and patron of a renaissance of Hebrew poetry and learning.
Constantinople's gift of a Greek Dioscorides was an instrument of statecraft, cultivating the western caliph as a counterweight and launching Cordoba's medical translation effort.
Otto I's envoy John of Gorze was kept waiting three years because his letter insulted Islam, a vivid glimpse of the Caliphate handling a rival's ambassador with bureaucratic patience.

title: The City of Books
bullets: Al-Hakam II wanted books, not conquest, and his caliphal library held a reputed hundreds of thousands of volumes when great Christian collections counted a few hundred manuscripts.
He funded twenty-seven free schools in Cordoba where the city's poor children were taught to read at the state's expense, with essentially no contemporary parallel in Latin Europe.
The library is the infrastructure of the intellectual transmission that is one of the two or three things al-Andalus is most genuinely important for in world history.
Al-Hakam built a new mihrab sheathed in gold mosaic by Greek Orthodox craftsmen sent from Constantinople by the Byzantine emperor, a Christian gift to a Muslim rival's mosque.
That single commission is real cross-civilizational collaboration, but it happened between rulers and elites as high diplomacy, not as a snapshot of street-level harmony.
When al-Hakam died in 976 the heir was an eleven-year-old, Hisham II, a child caliph in a state whose army now followed whoever paid it.

title: The Dictator and the Fall
bullets: Almanzor, a non-royal chamberlain, made himself absolute ruler by 981 while keeping Hisham II as a sacred figurehead he never deposed.
He launched some fifty near-annual razzias and lost almost none, sacking Barcelona in 985 and Santiago de Compostela in 997.
After Santiago he had the cathedral bells carried to Cordoba on Christian captives' shoulders; the famous mirror image of their later return north should be held more loosely.
To campaign every year he built an army of imported Berber regiments and saqaliba slave-soldiers loyal only to the paymaster, a short-term solution that was a long-term poison.
Almanzor died in 1002 and within seven years the structure detonated into the Fitna, a twenty-two-year multi-sided civil war.
Madinat al-Zahra was sacked and burned within eighty years of being built, which is why it is an archaeological site today and not a standing palace.
In 1031 Cordoba's leading citizens simply abolished the Caliphate; the most powerful state in the western Mediterranean had killed itself.

title: Thirty Kingdoms
bullets: The Caliphate fractured into roughly thirty rival taifa city-states, politically a catastrophe and culturally perhaps the most brilliant period al-Andalus ever had.
Every petty king wanted to be Cordoba and competed through poets and palaces, the same machine Renaissance Italy would run four hundred years later.
Ibn Hazm wrote The Ring of the Dove, the tenderest prose anatomy of love in Arabic, though he was a hardline literalist jurist, a signature al-Andalus paradox.
The parias were protection payments in gold from terrified taifa kings to Christian rulers, the mechanism by which the south paid the north to get strong enough to conquer it.
Alfonso VI took Toledo permanently in 1085, a capital-scale shock that handed the Christian world the city whose libraries became the great translation factory.
Asked to choose between Christian vassalage and the Saharan Almoravids, al-Mu'tamid of Seville reportedly said he would rather drive camels in Africa than herd swine in Castile.

title: The Veiled Men
bullets: The Almoravids were veiled Sanhaja Berber desert puritans from the western Sahara, summoned by the most refined court in the Islamic west to save it from Christian knights.
Yusuf ibn Tashfin crushed Alfonso VI at the Battle of Sagrajas, also called Zallaqa, in 1086, checking the Christian advance hard.
The same generation saw the Seljuks shatter Byzantium at Manzikert in 1071 and the First Crusade set out in 1095, a civilization-wide moment of stress.
Between 1090 and 1094 the Almoravids conquered the taifas, not the Christians, deposing the very kings who had invited them.
Al-Mu'tamid died a prisoner in the Moroccan oasis of Aghmat, writing exile poetry about his daughters in rags and the fetters on his own feet.
El Cid was a mercenary captain who fought for and against both faiths and ruled Valencia until 1099, sanded into a pure Christian hero only by the later poem.
The desert puritans grew comfortable within two generations, and the next desert movement would say of them exactly what they had said of the taifa kings.

title: The Unitarians
bullets: Ibn Tumart, an Atlas Berber preacher, declared himself the Mahdi and founded the Almohad movement around the absolute oneness of God, tawhid.
His successor Abd al-Mu'min, a commander of genius, destroyed the Almoravids, took Marrakesh in 1147, and built the largest single empire the Islamic west ever produced.
The Almohads largely abolished the dhimma, giving Jews and Christians conversion, exile, or death, and it is fleeing them that Maimonides left Cordoba.
Maimonides found safety and high office in the Cairo of Saladin, also a Muslim state: same religion, opposite policy, same generation.
The same fundamentalist century is the century of Averroes, Ibn Tufayl, and Ibn Arabi flourishing under Almohad patronage, sometimes the same court crushing the Jews.
Tolerant golden age and persecuting empire describe the same century and the same regime, which is exactly why the convivencia debate cannot be settled cheaply.
The Almohads built the Giralda of Seville and won big at Alarcos in 1195, their brief high-water mark before the Christian kingdoms finally united.

title: The Day the Map Broke
bullets: On July 16, 1212, the combined armies of Castile, Aragon, and Navarre destroyed the Almohad field army at Las Navas de Tolosa, the most decisive battle in the whole story.
Only eight years earlier a Catholic crusader army had sacked Christian Constantinople in 1204: the cross triumphant on one Mediterranean front and self-wounded on the other.
Cordoba fell in 1236, Valencia in 1238, and Seville in 1248; in about twenty-five years al-Andalus shrank to a single mountain kingdom around Granada.
The conquests created the Mudejars, Muslims under Christian rule, given a deal structurally identical to the dhimma, so convivencia ran both directions under both faiths.
The conquering Christians inherited cities full of Arabic books and Arabic-literate scholars, and in Toledo this became the most important translation enterprise in medieval Europe.
The political conquest of al-Andalus by Christian Europe is also the moment Europe absorbed al-Andalus's mind.

title: The Three Faiths
bullets: The dhimma was tolerated inequality in the original sense of toleration, permitted to exist, not regarded as equal, and the argument is always about which half to emphasize.
Samuel ibn Naghrillah was a practicing Jew who served as vizier and army commander of the taifa kingdom of Granada while being one of the great Hebrew poets of the age.
The Jewish Golden Age of Spain genuinely happened, but in 1066 the Muslim population of Granada massacred the Jewish community, the fact that most cleanly refutes the utopia reading.
Mark Cohen's comparison supports the synthesis: Jewish life under Islam was on average safer and freer than under contemporary Christendom, and also subordinate and never equal.
The Mozarabs under Muslim rule and the Mudejars under Christian rule lived the identical structure with the religions reversed, the single most clarifying fact in the debate.
The largest religious story of al-Andalus was not coexistence but conversion: after about 1000 the society was mostly the children of converts.
It was neither the candlelit interfaith garden nor an eight-century reign of terror, but a working multi-religious civilization built on inequality, like most premodern ones.

title: The House of Wisdom of the West
bullets: For three centuries al-Andalus was one of the few places that preserved and advanced ancient science, and the only such place in physical contact with Latin Europe.
The Toledo astronomer al-Zarqali compiled the Toledan Tables, the most accurate astronomical tables in the West for two centuries, cited into Copernicus's time.
Al-Zahrawi's illustrated surgical volume remained the standard surgical textbook in Europe for roughly six hundred years, into the 1700s.
The Arab Agricultural Revolution, a debated label for a real cluster of changes, brought rice, citrus, sugar, cotton, and eggplant into Europe through Spain with the irrigation to grow them.
Roughly eight percent of the Spanish vocabulary is of Arabic origin, the second-largest source after Latin and the most pervasive everyday fossil of al-Andalus.
Gerard of Cremona moved to Toledo because that was where the books were and personally translated some seventy works, including Ptolemy's Almagest.
Al-Andalus matters to science not because it had clever men but because it was the loading dock through which the ancient world re-entered the West.

title: The Commentator
bullets: Ibn Tufayl's Hayy ibn Yaqzan, a desert-island novel about whether unaided reason can reach truth, was written five and a half centuries before Robinson Crusoe.
Averroes wrote close commentaries recovering what Aristotle actually argued; to the Latin schoolmen he was simply the Commentator, capital C, no name needed.
His commentaries were a dead end in the later Islamic world but detonated in the Christian universities through the Toledo pipeline.
The system Aquinas built to reconcile Aristotle with Christianity, and the Latin Averroism the Church condemned in 1277, is unimaginable without the Cordoban judge.
Maimonides, born in Cordoba twelve years after Averroes, attempted the same Aristotelian synthesis for Judaism in the Guide for the Perplexed and influenced Aquinas directly.
Two of these philosophers, three religions, were born in the same Spanish city within a dozen years, all bolting Greek logic onto a revealed monotheism.
Ibn Arabi, the Greatest Master, carried Andalusi thought east into Sufism, so the civilization's mind ran in both directions, not only west into Christian Europe.

title: The Sound and the Look
bullets: The Great Mosque of Cordoba reuses Roman columns to say the new order inherits Rome, and stacks double-tiered horseshoe arches into a forest meant to feel like the infinity of God.
The horseshoe arch was a Visigothic form the conquerors adopted, evidence in stone that al-Andalus synthesized on the spot rather than importing wholesale from Damascus.
The courtyard garden built as an image of the Quranic paradise is the direct ancestor of the Spanish patio, the word included.
Andalusi poets created the muwashshah, whose closing kharja, often a woman's voice in early Romance, is among the oldest surviving lyric poetry in any Romance language.
Whether the kharja proves the Provencal troubadours were influenced by Andalusi song is a genuinely contested argument, suggestive adjacency but thin hard evidence.
Ziryab is credited with a fifth oud string and the restructuring of Cordoban dining, the kind of too-perfect one-man-civilized-them legend that should be held very loosely.
The buildings still standing are the clearest surviving evidence for the book's central claim: something genuinely new was made here, good enough that Europe still uses pieces of it.

title: The Last Kingdom
bullets: The Nasrid Emirate of Granada survived 254 years, longer than the Caliphate and the Almoravid and Almohad periods combined, not by being strong but by being clever.
Muhammad I became Ferdinand III's vassal in 1246 and helped a Christian army take Muslim Seville in 1248, the unsentimental foundation of the last paradise.
The survival strategy was a masterpiece of weak-state diplomacy: pay tribute, play the Marinids against Castile, exploit every Christian succession crisis, switch sides, endure.
The Alhambra achieves its overwhelming effect through cheap materials and surface alone, its walls covered in Quranic verse and original poetry by court poets.
Ibn Zamrak's verses on the Court of the Lions describe the very fountain they are carved beside, the building narrating itself to the person standing in it.
Ibn al-Khatib argued from observation that the Black Death spread by contagion, embodying a small client state still producing first-rate minds as everyone could see the map.
Ibn Battuta passed through in the 1350s and recorded a kingdom still confident and brilliant, with another century and a half to run.
The end came because of the 1469 marriage of Isabella and Ferdinand, the Catholic Monarchs, which united the two largest Christian crowns and ended the disunity Granada had exploited.
The Granada War ran 1482 to 1492 as a methodical town-by-town reduction with siege artillery the Nasrids could not match, helped by a three-way Nasrid civil war.
When Malaga resisted, its surviving population was killed or enslaved almost entirely, a deliberate demonstration of what resistance would cost.
On January 2, 1492, Boabdil handed over the keys of the Alhambra; 801 years after Tariq landed, no Muslim state remained in western Europe.
In March 1492 the Alhambra Decree expelled all Jews who would not convert, scattering the Sephardim, many welcomed by the Ottoman sultan Bayezid II.
That same spring the monarchs funded Christopher Columbus from the siege camp at Santa Fe; the same triumphant state did all three things in one year.
The end of the Iberian Middle Ages and the start of the European conquest of the world are, quite literally, the same season.

title: The Long Afterlife
bullets: The Capitulations of Granada lasted less than a decade before Cardinal Cisneros pushed mass forced conversion and Quran burnings, dismantling the promises.
The converted Muslims and their descendants became the Moriscos, Christian by law and suspected of secret Islam in fact, Spain's internal scapegoat for a century.
The War of the Alpujarras was crushed in 1571, and between 1609 and 1614 an estimated three hundred thousand Moriscos were expelled, the literal end of al-Andalus.
A history that stops at 1492 with Boabdil's sigh because the sigh is bearable and the expulsion is not is telling you something about the historian.
Washington Irving's 1832 Tales of the Alhambra fixed the modern romantic image of Moorish Spain and launched the tourism that now funds the building's survival.
Sephardic families kept Ladino alive and some kept the actual iron keys to the houses in Spain they had been expelled from, passed down for centuries.

What was lost was real and large; what is romanticized is also real; the truthful sentence is the uncomfortable one that al-Andalus was neither paradise nor prison but a brilliant civilization built on inequality.
